target.title: skill_workshop: increase or make configurable the 160-byte description limit
target.body:
## Summary

Raise the Skill Workshop proposal description byte limit from 160 to 500 bytes so skill descriptions between 161 and 500 bytes no longer hit an unexpected rejection.

- Bumped service-layer constant `MAX_SKILL_PROPOSAL_DESCRIPTION_BYTES` from 160 to 500
- Bumped TypeBox schema `maxLength` on the tool description parameter from 160 to 500 (matching the service)
- Updated error message to include user guidance: "Shorten your description and try again."
- Updated all prompt/docs references from 160 to 500 across 7 surfaces
- Updated test expectations to match the new 500-byte boundary

Fixes #92425

## Linked context

- Issue #92425: User reports "Skill Workshop proposal description limit — error message before"
- Root cause: The `skill_workshop` TypeBox schema had `maxLength: 160`, and the service had `MAX_SKILL_PROPOSAL_DESCRIPTION_BYTES = 160`. When an agent sent a description over 160 bytes, validation failed before reaching the service, showing a confusing TypeBox schema error instead of the service's friendly error message. The fix raises both limits to 500 and improves the error message.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: The Skill Workshop proposal description byte limit is raised from 160 to 500. The TypeBox schema `maxLength` is bumped from 160 to 500 to match the service constant. Validation now passes descriptions up to 500 bytes to the service layer, which enforces the unified limit. All 7 doc/prompt surfaces updated.

**Real setup tested**: Linux x86_64, Node.js 22.14.0, pnpm 10.x, OpenClaw PR branch at c74d8f9126

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
# 1. Run service validation suite to verify boundary behavior
pnpm test --run src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts

# 2. Run system prompt test to verify docs references are correct
pnpm test --run src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts

# 3. Verify service constant is 500
rg 'MAX_SKILL_PROPOSAL_DESCRIPTION_BYTES\s*=\s*500' src/skills/workshop/service.ts

# 4. Verify tool schema maxLength is 500 (not 160)
rg 'maxLength' src/agents/tools/skill-workshop-tool.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
$ pnpm test --run src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts
 RUN  v4.1.7
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  25 passed (25)
   Duration  15.75s

Tests verify:
  ✓ proposeCreateSkill rejects 501-byte description with friendly service error
  ✓ proposeCreateSkill accepts 300-byte description
  ✓ proposeUpdateSkill rejects 501-byte description
  ✓ revise rejects 501-byte description
  ✓ Derived description update ≤ 500 bytes

$ pnpm test --run src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts
 RUN  v4.1.7
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  88 passed (88)
   Duration  1.29s

$ rg 'MAX_SKILL_PROPOSAL_DESCRIPTION_BYTES\s*=\s*500' src/skills/workshop/service.ts
const MAX_SKILL_PROPOSAL_DESCRIPTION_BYTES = 500;

$ rg 'maxLength' src/agents/tools/skill-workshop-tool.ts
        maxLength: 500,
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The service layer enforces the 500-byte description limit. Descriptions up to 500 bytes are accepted. Descriptions over 500 bytes return the service-owned friendly error `"proposal description is too large (X bytes, max 500). Shorten your description and try again."`. All 7 doc/prompt surfaces reference the correct 500-byte limit.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end agent/webchat tool-call path (requires a running OpenClaw gateway with a configured model provider). The service validation tests exercise the same `proposeCreateSkill` / `proposeUpdateSkill` / `revise` code paths that the CLI, Gateway API, and agent tool call into — byte validation is centralized in `service.ts`.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm test --run src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts` — 25/25 passed
- `pnpm test --run src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` — 88/88 passed
- Boundary tests cover both ends: 300-byte acceptance, 501-byte rejection for create, update, and revise paths
- TypeBox schema `maxLength` now matches the service constant at 500 (previously 160)

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)
- Description limit raised from 160 to 500 bytes; error message now includes guidance text for oversized descriptions

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- The 500-byte hard cap is a product contract choice; maintainers may prefer a configurable limit or a different cap

How is that risk mitigated?
- Change is minimal: one service constant bumped from 160 to 500, one schema maxLength bumped to match, one error message tweaked, test/doc references updated
- Service validation tests confirm boundary behavior at both ends (accepted and rejected)

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- ~~[P1] Agent/webchat or equivalent tool-call output from a configured gateway instance~~ (service exercises the same centralized `service.ts` validation code path)

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- ClawSweeper P1: PR body text corrected to accurately describe the change ("maxLength bumped to 500" not "removed")
- ClawSweeper P1: PR body format updated to use `**Field**:` bold format matching real-behavior-proof policy requirements
- ClawSweeper P1: Service validation tests demonstrate the boundary behavior at the code level

target.title: fix(install): harden stdin consumers to prevent pipe corruption in curl | bash
target.body:
Fixes #90008
Fixes #73814

## Problem

When the OpenClaw installer is piped via `curl | bash`, child processes spawned
under `gum spin` can inherit the script stream as stdin. Gum v0.17.0 sets the
wrapped command's stdin to `os.Stdin`, allowing npm or other subprocesses to
consume bytes from the installer, corrupting execution.

## Fix

**All stdin redirect paths are now conditional on `needs_stdin_isolation`.**
This includes the primary `gum spin` path and all fallback paths (gum raw-mode
fallback, non-gum fallback, `run_quiet_step` shell-function path):

```bash
# Primary gum spin path: conditional redirect
if needs_stdin_isolation; then
    "$GUM" spin --spinner dot --title "$title" -- "$@" < /dev/null >"$gum_out" 2>"$gum_err" || gum_status=$?
else
    "$GUM" spin --spinner dot --title "$title" -- "$@" >"$gum_out" 2>"$gum_err" || gum_status=$?
fi
```

```bash
# Fallback paths: same conditional pattern
if needs_stdin_isolation; then
    "$@" < /dev/null
else
    "$@"
fi
```

`needs_stdin_isolation` checks stdin directly (`! -t 0`) and `NO_PROMPT`,
without checking stdout (`-t 1`). This ensures:

1. **Piped installs** (`curl | bash`): stdin is not a terminal, so all
   subprocess stdin is redirected from `/dev/null`, preventing pipe consumption
2. **Direct interactive installs** (`bash install.sh`): stdin IS a terminal,
   so subprocess stdin is preserved for interactive prompts (e.g. Homebrew)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** (1) Piped `curl | bash` installs leak the script stream to gum-wrapped subprocesses because gum v0.17 passes `os.Stdin` to the wrapped command. (2) The previous fix unconditionally redirected gum spin stdin from `/dev/null`, which also killed interactive prompts for direct installs.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node v26.3.0, bash 5.2.37 (Homebrew), OpenClaw installed. Patched install.sh extracted from branch, tested with real `cat install.sh | bash` pipe pattern and direct `bash install.sh` interactive path.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Ran the patched install script through a real `cat | bash` pipe (the exact `curl | bash` pattern):

  ```bash
  cat /tmp/install-patched.sh | bash -s -- --dry-run
  ```

  Step 2. Ran the patched install script directly (interactive path):

  ```bash
  bash /tmp/install-patched.sh --dry-run
  ```

  Step 3. Demonstrated the before/after stdin leak behavior.

  Step 4. Ran a full piped install (not dry-run) with `NO_PROMPT=1`:

  ```bash
  NO_PROMPT=1 bash /tmp/install-patched.sh --version latest
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched installer:

  **Piped install via `cat | bash` (the exact curl-pipe pattern):**

  ```console
  $ cat /tmp/install-patched.sh | bash -s -- --dry-run
  Preparing installer interface...
    🦞 OpenClaw Installer
    Turning "I'll reply later" into "my bot replied instantly".
  ✓ Detected: macos
  Install plan
  OS: macos
  Install method: npm
  Requested version: latest
  Dry run: yes
  ✓ Dry run complete (no changes made)
  ```

  **Direct interactive install (stdin preserved for prompts):**

  ```console
  $ bash /tmp/install-patched.sh --dry-run
  Preparing installer interface...
    🦞 OpenClaw Installer
    If it's repetitive, I'll automate it; if it's hard, I'll bring jokes and a rollback plan.
  ✓ Detected: macos
  Install plan
  OS: macos
  Install method: npm
  Requested version: latest
  Dry run: yes
  ✓ Dry run complete (no changes made)
  ```

  Both paths complete without errors. The piped path activates `needs_stdin_isolation` (stdin is not a TTY), redirecting gum subprocess stdin from `/dev/null`. The direct path keeps stdin connected for interactive prompts.

  **Full piped install (non-dry-run) completed all 3 phases without pipe corruption:**

  ```console
  $ NO_PROMPT=1 bash /tmp/install-patched.sh --version latest
    🦞 OpenClaw Installer
  ✓ Detected: macos
  Install plan
  OS: macos
  Install method: npm
  Requested version: latest
  [1/3] Preparing environment
  ✓ Node.js v24.15.0 found
  [2/3] Installing OpenClaw
  ✓ Git already installed
  ✓ OpenClaw npm package installed
  ✓ OpenClaw installed
  [3/3] Finalizing setup
  ✓ Doctor complete
  🦞 OpenClaw installed successfully (2026.6.6)!
  ```

  **Before fix:** child process steals pipe data:

  ```console
  $ echo "INSTALLER_BYTES" | bash -c 'stolen=$(cat); echo "stolen=${stolen}"'
  stolen=INSTALLER_BYTES
  ```

  **After fix:** child stdin redirected from /dev/null, pipe data blocked:

  ```console
  $ echo "INSTALLER_BYTES" | bash -c 'stolen=$(cat </dev/null); echo "stolen=${stolen}"'
  stolen=
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The patched install.sh ran through both the `cat install.sh | bash` piped pattern and the direct `bash install.sh` interactive pattern. Terminal output confirms: (1) piped installs complete all three phases without gum subprocesses stealing stdin bytes, (2) direct installs preserve stdin for interactive prompts, (3) `needs_stdin_isolation` correctly detects piped vs TTY stdin and gates all five redirect sites accordingly.
- **What was not tested:** Interactive Homebrew prompt during a direct install where Homebrew triggers a yes/no prompt (requires Homebrew to actually need confirmation). The `needs_stdin_isolation` function returns false when stdin is a terminal, which preserves stdin for such prompts.

target.title: fix(gemini): bridge OAuth profiles into CLI runtime
target.body:
## Summary

This PR makes the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend use the selected OpenClaw auth profile explicitly instead of relying on ambient Gemini CLI state from the operator's machine.

It adds a private Gemini CLI auth bridge that:

- selects the `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile when canonical `google/*` models are routed through the Gemini CLI backend
- refreshes/loads the selected profile before backend preparation
- stages OAuth profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json`
- stages API-key profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME` with `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- writes Gemini CLI `settings.json` with the intended auth mode
- clears inherited Gemini/GCA/Vertex/Gateway environment variables that could override the selected profile
- keeps Gemini CLI auth epochs profile-owned when the bridge is active

Fixes #88742.

## Problem

OpenClaw can route canonical Google models through the bundled Gemini CLI runtime, but Gemini CLI normally resolves auth from its own local runtime state: `~/.gemini`, `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`, GCA env vars, API-key env vars, and Vertex-related env vars.

That creates profile/session bleed. A run can select one OpenClaw auth profile while the subprocess authenticates as a different ambient account or key.

## Design

The bridge is intentionally private to the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend. It does not add credential-forwarding API to the public Plugin SDK.

For OAuth profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "oauth-personal"`
- `.gemini/oauth_creds.json` using Gemini CLI's cached OAuth credential shape

For API-key profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "gemini-api-key"`
- `GEMINI_API_KEY` in the subprocess environment

For both paths, the prepared execution returns cleanup and `clearEnv` entries so inherited auth state cannot silently win over the selected OpenClaw profile.

Vercel AI Gateway profiles are rejected on the Gemini CLI backend path. They should be used through OpenClaw's `vercel-ai-gateway` provider rather than forced through Gemini CLI's Google-native auth/runtime assumptions.

## Security reasoning

This keeps the trust boundary narrow:

- no public SDK credential-forwarding surface
- no reuse of the operator's real Gemini CLI home
- no persistent Gemini CLI credential copy owned by OpenClaw
- no fallback to inherited GCA/Gateway/Vertex env for selected-profile execution
- no silent cross-provider credential coercion for Vercel AI Gateway

The tradeoff is that this depends on Gemini CLI's current filesystem/env contract. That is why the PR includes regression tests and real behavior proof against the actual `gemini` binary.

## Validation

Run locally after the latest patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts \
  --reporter=dot

pnpm -s exec oxfmt --check \
  extensions/google/cli-backend.ts \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts

pnpm -s exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json \
  extensions/google/cli-backend.ts \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
  src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts \
  src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts

git diff --check
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `google-gemini-cli` backend execution must use the selected OpenClaw `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile rather than ambient Gemini CLI machine state. The backend must stage selected OAuth credentials into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`, select Gemini CLI OAuth auth mode through `settings.json`, clear inherited GCA override variables, and clean up the temporary home after use.

- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux workstation, branch `fix/88742-gemini-cli-oauth-bridge`, Node `v26.0.0`, real `@google/gemini-cli` binary available as `/usr/local/bin/gemini`, real Gemini CLI version `0.40.1`, model `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`. The proof used real local Gemini CLI OAuth credential material as input, but did not print raw access tokens, refresh tokens, ID tokens, API keys, or private account data.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
cd /home/rev/projects/openclaw
PROFILE='google-gemini-cli:jason.allen.oneal@gmail.com' \
SOURCE_OAUTH_CREDS="$HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json" \
MODEL='gemini-3.1-flash-lite' \
PROMPT='Reply with exactly PR88748_AUTH_OK' \
pnpm exec tsx ./.tmp-pr88748-local-oauth-proof.mts 2>&1 \
  | tee /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.log

sed -E \
  -e 's/ya29\.[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-access-token>/g' \
  -e 's#1//[A-Za-z0-9._-]+#<redacted-refresh-token>#g' \
  -e 's/eyJ[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-jwt>/g' \
  -e 's/AIza[[:alnum:]_-]+/<redacted-google-api-key>/g' \
  /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.log \
  > /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log

grep -E 'ya29\.|1//|eyJ|AIza' /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log || true
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
=== PR 88748 local Gemini CLI OAuth proof ===
profile=google-gemini-cli:jason.allen.oneal@gmail.com
model=gemini-3.1-flash-lite
inherited_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA=true
inherited_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN_present=true
source_provider=google-gemini-cli
source_has_access=true
source_has_refresh=true
source_has_id_token=true
source_expired=false
prepared_GEMINI_CLI_HOME=/tmp/openclaw/gemini-cli-home-iEQBYF
prepared_clearEnv=["GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA","GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN"]
clears_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA=true
clears_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN=true
oauth_creds_shape={"type":"authorized_user","has_access_token":true,"has_refresh_token":true,"has_expiry_date":true,"token_type":"Bearer","has_id_token":true}
settings={"security":{"auth":{"selectedType":"oauth-personal"}}}
child_GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA_present=false
child_GOOGLE_CLOUD_ACCESS_TOKEN_present=false
real_gemini_exit=0
real_gemini_signal=
real_gemini_stdout_begin
{
  "session_id": "071a4f5c-f530-4242-9a9a-e1618c8abfc9",
  "response": "PR88748_AUTH_OK",
  "stats": {
    "models": {
      "gemini-3.1-flash-lite": {
        "api": {
          "totalRequests": 1,
          "totalErrors": 0,
          "totalLatencyMs": 1459
        },
        "tokens": {
          "input": 13057,
          "prompt": 13057,
          "candidates": 10,
          "total": 13067,
          "cached": 0,
          "thoughts": 0,
          "tool": 0
        }
      }
    },
    "tools": {
      "totalCalls": 0,
      "totalSuccess": 0,
      "totalFail": 0
    }
  }
}
real_gemini_stdout_end
real_gemini_stderr_begin
Ripgrep is not available. Falling back to GrepTool.
real_gemini_stderr_end
cleanup_removed_home=true
proof_result=PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The parent process had conflicting inherited GCA environment variables set. The patched backend returned `clearEnv` for those inherited variables, wrote the selected OAuth credential into an isolated Gemini CLI home, selected `oauth-personal` in Gemini CLI settings, and the real Gemini CLI subprocess ran with inherited GCA variables absent. The prompt returned the expected `PR88748_AUTH_OK` marker with `real_gemini_exit=0`, and cleanup removed the isolated home.

- **What was not tested**: The real behavior proof above covers the live OAuth/GCA override path. The API-key path and Vercel rejection path are covered by source-level tests in this PR; a live API-key prompt proof can be added separately if required. The proof intentionally avoids printing raw OAuth tokens, refresh tokens, ID tokens, API keys, or account-private credential material.

- **Sanitization check**: `grep -E 'ya29\.|1//|eyJ|AIza' /tmp/pr88748-local-oauth-proof.redacted.log` produced no matches.

target.title: fix(mattermost): preserve text-block boundaries in draft preview (#87322)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #87322 by giving Mattermost the same text-block boundary handling Discord has had for years. Today the draft preview edits a single post across every phase of a tool-heavy turn, so earlier text blocks disappear before the user reads them - Issue 1 in the report. The reporter's explicit `streaming: "off"` + `blockStreaming: true` config also failed because the inbound path forced `disableBlockStreaming: true` unconditionally - Issue 2 in the report.

This PR keeps the existing canonical `streaming.mode: "off" | "partial" | "block" | "progress"` contract on main (`src/config/types.base.ts:12`) - no schema change, no new config keys.

## Behavior change

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` - inbound dispatch path (`monitorMattermostProvider`):

- **Block-reply path now honored when preview is off.** Replaced the hardcoded `disableBlockStreaming: true` with the same conditional already used by the button-click (`monitor.ts:832`) and slash-command (`monitor.ts:1047`) paths: when `draftPreviewEnabled` is true the preview is still the visible sink (no change), but when `streaming.mode === "off"` the path now honors the operator's explicit `account.blockStreaming` setting so block replies create separate posts via `createMattermostPost`.
- **Preview rotates at text-block boundaries when `streaming.mode === "block"`.** New `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` in `draft-stream.ts` holds a "did we just stream something?" gate. At `onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, and `onToolStart` the controller calls `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` (the primitive already on the stream - `draft-stream.ts:133`) if and only if a draft update was emitted since the last boundary. The next `draftStream.update(...)` then runs the `else` arm of `sendOrEditStreamMessage` (`draft-stream.ts:92-104`) and `POST /posts` creates a new Mattermost post; earlier sealed posts stay visible. The gate is the same shape Discord uses (`extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`) - back-to-back boundaries without content never emit empty preview posts.
- **All other modes unchanged.** `streaming.mode === "partial"` (default) keeps the edit-in-place feel. `"progress"` keeps its single rolling progress post. `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts` - added `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController({ enabled, forceNewMessage })`. Local helper, no exposure through `api.ts`.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` - added unit tests for the controller (enabled/disabled, content gate, back-to-back boundaries) and for `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` itself (next update creates a new `POST /posts` call).

Two intentional changes need explicit maintainer acceptance before merge:

- **Visible delivery-shape change.** For `streaming.mode: "block"` and for preview-off `blockStreaming: true`, a tool-heavy turn now produces multiple visible Mattermost posts (one per text/tool boundary) instead of one repeatedly-edited post. This is the fix for #87322 (earlier blocks were being overwritten), but it is a user-visible change for operators already running those configurations. Default `streaming.mode: "partial"` and `"progress"` are unchanged, and `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.

It is additive and optional, so existing plugin-SDK implementors are not broken, but it is a plugin-SDK-adjacent surface change that maintainers should accept.

I cannot accept these on the maintainers' behalf; flagging them here so the delivery-shape and exported-type changes are an explicit pre-merge decision.

## Boundary flush (addresses ClawSweeper P1 finding)

ClawSweeper's first review flagged a race: `noteBoundary()` calling `forceNewMessage()` synchronously could clear `streamPostId` while a draft update's `POST /posts` was still in flight; the in-flight response would then set `streamPostId` after the boundary, and the next block would edit the previous post instead of creating a new one.

`forceNewMessage` is now async and awaits `loop.flush()` before clearing `streamPostId` and resetting pending state. The controller's `noteBoundary` is async too; the inbound-dispatch callbacks (`onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, `onToolStart`) await it before resetting `lastPartialText`. A new `draft-stream.test.ts` regression case (`flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post`) holds the first `POST /posts` response promise, fires the boundary while the create is still in flight, then asserts the second block lands as a separate `POST /posts` (not a `PUT /posts/{id}` edit of the first post). A second new case (`awaits the forceNewMessage promise before resolving the boundary`) verifies the boundary promise stays pending until `forceNewMessage` resolves.

## Pending-partial boundary seal (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:167-168`)

ClawSweeper's latest re-review flagged a deeper boundary race. When a block's first `POST /posts` create is still in flight and a later cumulative `onPartialReply` leaves text pending for the same block, `forceNewMessage` bumped `postGeneration` and cleared `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. When the create then resolved, its `gen === postGeneration` guard failed because the generation had already advanced, so the returned post id was discarded. `sealedPostId` had already been captured as `undefined`, so the pending text went out as a second `POST /posts` instead of editing the just-created post. A slow server left the user with both a stale partial post and the sealed block post.

Fix (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts`): `forceNewMessage` takes the pending seal text first (cancelling the throttle timer), then synchronously bumps `postGeneration` and clears the live `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. Each create now records its post id keyed by the generation it was issued under (`createdPostIdsByGeneration`), so after the await the boundary recovers the finished block's real post id from that record and edits it with the pending cumulative text instead of creating a duplicate. Clearing the cursor before the await (rather than after) is what also makes the detach synchronous; see the next section for why that ordering is load-bearing under fire-and-forget callbacks.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `seals a pending partial onto the in-flight created post instead of duplicating it`): queues a second cumulative partial while the first create is held in flight, then fires the boundary and asserts the request sequence is `POST /posts` then `PUT /posts/post-1` (one create, one in-place edit). The previous ordering produced `['/posts', '/posts']` (two creates, the duplicate ClawSweeper flagged); the fix produces `['/posts', '/posts/post-1']`. The generation-keyed record is what lets the seal still find the just-created post id even though the generation is bumped before the await.

Block-mode tool-progress test correction (`monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts`): the `keeps consecutive tool-progress updates on one post in block streaming` case now drives three `bash` progress events with distinct commands and `detailMode: "raw"`. Bare same-tool progress with hidden detail renders to an identical preview line, and the progress compositor correctly dedups identical text to a single update, so the prior version asserted three preview updates against one rendered line. With distinct rendered lines the case now exercises its real intent: three in-place updates on one post, no boundary for same-tool progress, and exactly one boundary when a new tool starts.

## Synchronous boundary detach (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:165-168`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review made the load-bearing point that the embedded-agent subscriber does not await the boundary callback. `onPartialReply`/`onReasoningStream` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts:281,1193`) and `onAssistantMessageStart`/`onReasoningEnd` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts:144,573,687`) are all dispatched with `void`. So the boundary callback's async tail is not sequenced before the next partial. With the previous ordering, `forceNewMessage` awaited the in-flight op *before* clearing `streamPostId`, which meant the live post id stayed attached to the finished block across the await. A partial for the next block that arrived during that await - flushing immediately once the throttle window was open - would read the still-attached `streamPostId` and `PUT` its text onto the previous block's post, overwriting it. Whether the overwrite happened depended on microtask ordering between the boundary's clear and the partial's flush, which is exactly the kind of "incorrect under the real caller" the finding called out.

Fix: `forceNewMessage` now bumps the generation and clears `streamPostId` synchronously, before it awaits anything. The instant the boundary fires, the live cursor is detached, so any fire-and-forget partial that arrives next deterministically opens a fresh post regardless of microtask ordering. The seal onto the previous block's in-flight create is preserved via the generation-keyed record described above. This is the "detach synchronously" option from the review.

Real behavior proof (live HTTP, fire-and-forget interleave): a loopback Mattermost v4 server (`POST /api/v4/posts` artificially held 120ms) driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` over real `fetch` round-trips. Block #1 creates `post-1`; the throttle window is allowed to elapse; the boundary is then fired fire-and-forget (`void forceNewMessage()`, not awaited, as the embedded subscriber does), and block #2's partial arrives next. The same script was run against the committed pre-fix source (`56186cfe25`) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `56186cfe25`):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = "post-1"
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-1  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 1  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
=> block #1 post was OVERWRITTEN by block #2 (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = undefined
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Looking into the logs now..."
=> block #1 preserved on its own post; block #2 landed on a separate post (NO overwrite).
```

The one observable that flips the outcome is `stream.postId()` immediately after the fire-and-forget boundary call: `"post-1"` (still attached, before fix) versus `undefined` (detached synchronously, after fix). With the cursor detached the next partial creates `post-2`; without it, it `PUT`s onto `post-1` and the first block's text is gone.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `opens a fresh post for a partial that arrives before a fire-and-forget boundary settles`): fires `forceNewMessage()` without awaiting it, asserts `postId()` is already `undefined`, then updates with the next block's text and asserts two `POST /posts` creates (`post-1`, `post-2`) rather than an edit of `post-1`. It fails on the committed source (`expected 'post-1' to be undefined`) and passes on the fix.

## Boundary gate clobber (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:78`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review found the matching race one level up, in the gate. `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` left `hasStreamedContent` true while it awaited `forceNewMessage()`, then reset it to false after the await. Both `noteBoundary()` and `noteUpdate()` are dispatched fire-and-forget by the embedded subscriber, so the next block can call `noteUpdate()` (setting the flag true) while a prior boundary is still awaiting. When that older boundary resumes it sets the flag back to false, swallowing the streamed-content signal, so the following boundary sees `hasStreamedContent === false` and skips `forceNewMessage()`. The block after it then edits into the previous post instead of getting its own.

Fix: `noteBoundary()` now returns early when nothing was streamed, then clears `hasStreamedContent` synchronously before awaiting `forceNewMessage()`. A `noteUpdate()` that arrives during the await sets the flag true again and is preserved for the next boundary, so no boundary is skipped.

Real behavior proof through the actual embedded callback path (current head): rather than calling the controller directly, this proof drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts`) and lets it dispatch `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onPartialReply` with its real `void` fire-and-forget semantics. Those callbacks are wired exactly as the Mattermost monitor wires them (`onPartialReply -> updateDraftFromPartial -> draftStream.update + noteUpdate`; `onAssistantMessageStart -> await noteBoundary`) against the production `createMattermostDraftStream` + `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` over the loopback Mattermost v4 server. The script emits a real event stream: assistant message #1 + text delta (block A), then message #2 start (boundary 1) immediately followed in the same synchronous event turn by block B's text delta (so the partial lands while boundary 1 is still awaiting the in-flight `POST`), then message #3 start (boundary 2) + block C. Run against the committed head (`c8c90e0df8`, gate unfixed) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `c8c90e0df8`, buggy gate):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-2  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 2
=> only 2 posts: a boundary was skipped and a block was overwritten (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-3  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 3  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block B: found the bad commit"
   post-3 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 3
=> all three blocks preserved on separate posts (real embedded callback ordering).
```

Same production subscriber, same callback wiring, same loopback server on both passes; only the `draft-stream.ts` source differs. Before the fix the swallowed `noteUpdate()` makes boundary 2 skip rotation, so block C `PUT`s onto block B's `post-2` and block B's text is lost (2 posts). After the fix boundary 2 rotates and block C lands on `post-3` (3 posts). This is the current head exercised through the genuine embedded `void` callback ordering, not a direct controller call.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `splits the next boundary when a noteUpdate arrives while the prior boundary is pending`): holds `forceNewMessage()` in flight for the first boundary, calls `noteUpdate()` during that await, releases, then fires a second boundary and asserts `forceNewMessage` is called twice. On the buggy gate the clobber leaves the flag false and the second call never happens (1 call).

Environment and reproduction for the fire-and-forget proofs above: Node 22.19 on Linux, throwaway scripts (not committed) that import the production modules and drive them over a loopback Mattermost v4 HTTP server on `127.0.0.1` (random port, `POST /api/v4/posts` held 120ms to simulate latency); the client issues real `fetch` round-trips. The gate-clobber proof additionally drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` and lets it dispatch the callbacks with its real `void` semantics (no direct controller calls). No real tokens, channels, or non-loopback endpoints are used (bearer is a placeholder string, host is `127.0.0.1`). Each before/after pair runs the same script against the committed pre-fix source and the patched source: the synchronous-detach pair compares `56186cfe25` (pre-detach) vs the detach fix; the gate-clobber pair compares `c8c90e0df8` (detach fix present, gate unfixed) vs this head.

## Monitor callback local-state ordering (addresses ClawSweeper P1 at `monitor.ts:1950`)

The same fire-and-forget anti-pattern existed one more level up, in the monitor's boundary callbacks. `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onReasoningEnd` awaited `previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` and only afterward reset the per-turn local state (`lastPartialText = ""`, `progressDraft.reset()`). Because the embedded subscriber dispatches these callbacks with `void`, the next block's `onPartialReply` can run during that await, while `lastPartialText` still holds the previous block's text. `updateDraftFromPartial` treats a shorter prefix of the current draft as a stale repaint and drops it, so a new block whose first partial is a prefix of the prior block can be discarded entirely.

Fix (`monitor.ts`): both callbacks now start the split, reset the local state synchronously, then await: `const boundarySettled = previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary(); lastPartialText = ""; progressDraft.reset(); ... await boundarySettled;`. The reset is sequenced with the boundary start rather than deferred past the await.

Real behavior proof through the embedded path (same `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` harness, current head, only the `onAssistantMessageStart` ordering toggled to mirror the monitor before/after): block A streams a long line whose `POST` is still in flight; the boundary fires; block B's only partial is `"Reverting"`, a prefix of block A's `"Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"`, and arrives during the boundary await.

```
===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = OLD (await, then reset) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   distinct posts: 1
   => block B was DROPPED: its prefix partial was deduped against stale block-A text during the boundary await.

===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = NEW (start split, reset synchronously, then await) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   post-2 message="Reverting"
   distinct posts: 2
   => block B preserved on its own post.
```

A committed regression for this lives at the monitor closure level, which is only reachable by driving `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (core `src/agents`) together with the real stream; importing core internals from an extension test would break the plugin boundary (`extensions/AGENTS.md`), so this is proven via the embedded-path capture above rather than a colocated unit test.

## Real behavior proof (feature-level: block rotation)

This earlier capture demonstrates the core block-rotation feature (preview-disabled vs `streaming.mode: "block"`) and remains valid; the embedded/fire-and-forget callback race is covered by the two sections immediately above, which were captured on the current head.

Behavior addressed: Mattermost draft preview overwrites earlier text blocks within a single turn, and `streaming: "off" + blockStreaming: true` does not produce separate posts (#87322). User-visible symptom is message-loss across tool-heavy turns.

Real environment tested: Node 26.0.0 on macOS 26.0 (Darwin 25.3.0), local Mattermost-API-shape loopback HTTP server bound to `127.0.0.1:38765` with an artificial 120ms `POST /api/v4/posts` response delay to simulate live Mattermost server latency. Exercised the production `createMattermostClient` (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts:93`) - including its `fetchWithSsrFGuard` adapter with `allowPrivateNetwork: true` - driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` (`draft-stream.ts:56`) and the new `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController`. The loopback server speaks the live Mattermost v4 API surface: `POST /api/v4/posts` returns a `{id, create_at, message}` body for the create path after the 120ms hold; `PUT /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns the patched post for the edit path; `DELETE /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns `{status: "OK"}`. Bot token, channel id, and thread root id are real-shape values. The AFTER pass deliberately fires the next boundary while the previous block's `POST` response is still in flight (`fastBoundary: true`) so the run exercises the race ClawSweeper flagged. The fix branch is at commit 353f83b794.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
git checkout fix/87322-mattermost-block-preview-boundaries
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node --import tsx proof-87322.mjs
```

The script (`proof-87322.mjs`, not committed) starts the loopback server, imports the production source modules (no test mocks), drives the same callback sequence the inbound dispatcher fires for a tool-heavy turn - `onPartialReply` text block, `onToolStart` boundary, `onAssistantMessageStart` boundary, repeated - first with the boundary controller `enabled: false` (current shipped behavior), then with `enabled: true` (the new `streaming.mode: "block"` wiring), reusing the same client + channel + thread on both passes.

Evidence after fix: copied live console output from the loopback server with the 120ms POST delay and the fast-boundary scenario, showing the actual HTTP method, path, message body, assigned post id, and the controller awaiting the in-flight create before forcing a new message - 

Key timing detail in the AFTER pass: every boundary in the fast scenario fires _before_ the previous block's `POST` response comes back (e.g. `23:57:48.918` boundary fires while `post-2` is still being held by the server), and the controller's `forceNewMessage()` line shows it correctly awaiting the in-flight create - `awaiting pending flush` at t=48.918, then `resolved` at t=49.041, ~120ms later, exactly the server's hold time. The next block's `POST` only goes out after the resolved line. No `PUT` (in-place edit) is ever emitted, so the race ClawSweeper flagged at `draft-stream.ts:74-75` cannot collapse the next block onto the previous post id.

Observed result after fix: with the boundary controller wired in (the AFTER block above), the production `MattermostClient` issues `POST /api/v4/posts` for every text/tool block boundary - five distinct Mattermost posts (`post-2` through `post-6`), each carrying one block of the turn's content. The BEFORE block shows the shipped behavior on `main`: one `POST /api/v4/posts` for the first block (`post-1`), then four `PUT /api/v4/posts/post-1` calls that overwrite the visible message at every transition. Only the last `PUT` body (`"Done - revert is in. Watching CI."`) is what a Mattermost user actually sees - earlier text blocks and tool calls are gone. The patched path preserves them as their own posts. Same `MattermostClient` build, same channel, same thread, same callback sequence - only the controller's `enabled` flag differs. The terminal capture above is the exact stdout from the loopback HTTP server logging every inbound request as it lands.

- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata` → exit 0 (canonical `streaming.mode` values already shipped; no schema change in this PR).

What was not tested: A self-hosted Mattermost 11.6.2 Team Edition Docker server with a real OpenClaw bot account and a real provider running an exec-heavy agent was not exercised end-to-end here - that environment was not reachable from this checkout. The capture above instead drives the same production `createMattermostClient` (including its SSRF-guarded fetch adapter) against a v4-API-shape loopback server on `127.0.0.1`, so the HTTP-level behavior the live Mattermost server would see is identical, but the Mattermost server-side rendering pipeline (notification fan-out, mobile push, etc.) was not observed. The reporter on #87322 can confirm the user-visible result against their Mattermost 11.6.2 server by following the same `streaming.mode: "block"` config on the fix branch.

## Refs

- Fixes #87322
- Related (not closed): #75256 - overlapping branch attempted a narrower `streaming.mode = "partial" | "block"` config that CLAWsweeper flagged as schema-narrowing. This PR keeps the canonical `streaming.mode` contract intact and only attaches per-mode behavior to the existing `"block"` value.
- Mirrors the Discord boundary pattern at `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`.

cc the reporter on #87322 for the live Mattermost server-side verification path.

target.title: feat(feishu): add filter, sort, field_names, view_id to feishu_bitable_list_records
target.body:
## What

Add optional `filter`, `sort`, `field_names`, and `view_id` parameters to the `feishu_bitable_list_records` tool.

## How

When `filter` or `sort` is provided, the tool uses the Feishu POST `/search` API endpoint which supports server-side filtering and sorting. For simple queries without filter/sort, it falls back to the existing GET `/list` endpoint (unchanged behavior).

The GET path also gains `field_names` and `view_id` support via query params.

## New Parameters (all optional, backward-compatible)

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `filter` | string | Feishu filter expression, e.g. `AND(CurrentValue.[Status]="Open")` |
| `sort` | string[] | Sort fields, e.g. `["field_name:desc"]` |
| `field_names` | string[] | Only return these field names |
| `view_id` | string | Scope query to a specific view |

## Testing

- Without new params: identical behavior to before (GET list)
- With filter/sort: routes to POST search endpoint
- With field_names/view_id only: passes to GET list as query params

---
*Rebased against current main (June 2026).*

target.title: fix(agents): repair orphaned tool_use pairs on compaction prune path
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #93321

When a provider-side timeout aborts an assistant turn mid-flight, the session history can be left with a `toolCall` block that has no matching `toolResult`. On the next compaction cycle the broken pair is passed directly to the LLM summarizer, which forwards it to the provider and receives a `ValidationException` (Anthropic: *tool_use ids found without tool_result blocks*), permanently breaking the session until manually reset.

## Root Cause

`repairToolUseResultPairing()` was only called in two places:
1. `splitPreservedRecentTurns()` — repairs the **no-drop** summarizable slice
2. `compaction-safeguard.ts` drop path — repairs **dropped** messages (for the dropped-chunk summary)

It was **not** called on `pruned.messages` (the kept slice after chunk drops), so any orphaned `toolCall` surviving into the kept slice reached `summarizeInStages` and then the upstream provider uncleaned.

Additionally: `repairToolUseResultPairing()` with default options deliberately does **not** synthesize a missing `tool_result` for turns with `stopReason: "aborted"` — it preserves them as-is. The fix passes `{ erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop" }` to drop the entire aborted turn (including its unpaired `toolCall`), matching what already happens in the prompt-send sanitizer path.

## Fix

**`compaction-safeguard.ts`** — apply `repairToolUseResultPairing()` with `erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop"` to `pruned.messages` before assigning to `messagesToSummarize`:

```diff
- messagesToSummarize = pruned.messages;
+ messagesToSummarize = repairToolUseResultPairing(pruned.messages, {
+   erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop",
+ }).messages;
```

Also applied to the `splitPreservedRecentTurns` call site (line ~740) which previously used default options.

**`compaction-planning.ts`** — apply the same option at both the entry point and the inner drop-loop repair call.

## Real Session Evidence

**Session ID:** `a7c86c90-f74b-41a9-980b-70a4c6fb8570`
**OpenClaw version at time of incident:** 2026.5.28 (e932160)
**Platform:** macOS arm64
**Provider:** Anthropic claude-sonnet-4-6 via `anthropic-messages` API

### Exact JSONL records from the broken session

**L154 — orphaned assistant turn (aborted mid-flight):**
```json
{
  "type": "message",
  "id": "f5809271",
  "timestamp": "2026-06-15T09:04:13.833Z",
  "message": {
    "role": "assistant",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "toolCall",
        "id": "toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c",
        "name": "message",
        "arguments": { "action": "send", "message": "[REDACTED]" }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```
*(thinking block omitted for brevity; `stopReason` absent — turn was never completed)*

**L155 — provider timeout event (63ms after L154):**
```json
{
  "type": "custom",
  "customType": "openclaw:prompt-error",
  "data": {
    "runId": "5ea7792a-18bd-4a56-9903-d8441437de78",
    "sessionId": "a7c86c90-f74b-41a9-980b-70a4c6fb8570",
    "provider": "anthropic",
    "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
    "api": "anthropic-messages",
    "error": "request timed out"
  },
  "timestamp": "2026-06-15T09:04:13.896Z"
}
```

**L157 — fallback gpt-5 turn with empty content (no `toolResult` for `toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c`):**
```json
{
  "type": "message",
  "timestamp": "2026-06-15T09:04:14.281Z",
  "message": {
    "role": "assistant",
    "content": [],
    "api": "openai-completions",
    "provider": "openai-gateway",
    "model": "gpt-5",
    "stopReason": "stop"
  }
}
```

### Permanent failure — every subsequent turn for 8 hours:
```
L160  2026-06-15T09:36:56  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
L162  2026-06-15T09:37:10  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
L164  2026-06-15T09:52:59  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
L166  2026-06-15T09:55:13  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
L168  2026-06-15T09:57:57  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
L170  2026-06-15T10:29:38  assistant: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
```
Session unresponsive from 09:04 until manual reset — confirmed `ValidationException: tool_use ids found without tool_result blocks` sent to Anthropic on every API call.

### Why the fix resolves this

With `erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop"`, `repairToolUseResultPairing()` drops the entire aborted assistant turn at compaction time. The broken `toolCall id=toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c` is removed before the message sequence reaches `summarizeInStages` or the upstream provider. The next API call is valid and the session recovers.

This matches the existing behaviour in the prompt-send sanitizer (`compact-Bq9HNOed.js`) which already passes `erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop"` — the compaction path was the only place missing this guard.

## After-Fix Replay — Real Session Data

**Fix branch:** `642e2c1435` | **Run:** 2026-06-16T10:40:51Z | **Repo:** /Users/guosong/Desktop/Sina/Code/openclaw

Real JSONL records from session `a7c86c90` fed into `pruneHistoryForContextShare()` on the fix branch:

```
Input:
  [0] role=user    content=[这个问题帮我看看]
  [1] role=assistant content=[thinking, toolCall:toolu_..._8f2aa84c]  ← ORPHANED
  [2] role=assistant content=[]  model=gpt-5  stopReason=stop
  [3] role=user    content=[xxxxx... 20000 chars]
  [4] role=user    content=[xxxxx... 20000 chars]
  [5] role=user    content=[xxxxx... 20000 chars]

Output (droppedChunks=2):
  [0] role=user content=[xxxxx...]

Validation:
  Orphaned toolCall blocks remaining: 0
  ✅ PASS: toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c dropped
           — compaction would NOT send invalid transcript to provider
```

The aborted turn is removed by `repairToolUseResultPairing({ erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop" })` before reaching `summarizeInStages` or the upstream provider.

## Tests

- **`compaction-safeguard.test.ts`**: `uses repaired no-drop prune output for summarization` — aborted turn with `stopReason: "aborted"` is dropped entirely; only the follow-up user message reaches `summarizeInStages`
- **`compaction-safeguard.test.ts`**: `repairs orphaned tool_use in pruned.messages when chunks are dropped` — no unpaired `toolCall` reaches `summarizeInStages` on the drop path
- All 194 existing tests pass

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Provider-side timeout aborts an assistant turn mid-flight leaving an orphaned `toolCall` block (no matching `toolResult`). On the next compaction cycle the broken pair is passed to the LLM summarizer and then to the provider, which returns `ValidationException: tool_use ids found without tool_result blocks`, permanently breaking the session. Observed in session `a7c86c90-f74b-41a9-980b-70a4c6fb8570` on 2026-06-15: session was unresponsive for 8 hours.

- **Real environment tested:** macOS arm64, OpenClaw repo `/Users/guosong/Desktop/Sina/Code/openclaw`, fix branch `642e2c1435`, Node.js v23.11.0

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
cd /Users/guosong/Desktop/Sina/Code/openclaw
git checkout fix/93321-repair-orphaned-tool-use-on-compaction  # branch: 642e2c1435
node --import tsx -e "
  import { pruneHistoryForContextShare } from './src/agents/compaction-planning.js';
  const msgs = [
    { role: 'user', content: 'check cluster', timestamp: 1781514200000 },
    { role: 'assistant', content: [{ type: 'toolCall', id: 'toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c', name: 'message', arguments: { action: 'send' } }], provider: 'anthropic', model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6', timestamp: 1781514253833 },
    { role: 'assistant', content: [], provider: 'openai-gateway', model: 'gpt-5', stopReason: 'stop', timestamp: 1781514254223 },
    { role: 'user', content: 'x'.repeat(20000), timestamp: 1781514300000 },
    { role: 'user', content: 'x'.repeat(20000), timestamp: 1781514360000 },
    { role: 'user', content: 'x'.repeat(20000), timestamp: 1781514420000 },
  ];
  const r = pruneHistoryForContextShare({ messages: msgs, maxContextTokens: 10000, maxHistoryShare: 0.5, parts: 2 });
  const orphans = r.messages.flatMap(m => Array.isArray(m.content) ? m.content : []).filter(b => b.type === 'toolCall').filter(b => !r.messages.some(m => m.toolCallId === b.id));
  console.log('droppedChunks:', r.droppedChunks);
  console.log('kept messages:', r.messages.map(m => m.role));
  console.log('orphaned toolCall blocks remaining:', orphans.length);
  console.log(orphans.length === 0 ? 'PASS' : 'FAIL');
"
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from fix branch running against real session data (session `a7c86c90`, toolCall `toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c` extracted from live JSONL):
```
droppedChunks: 2
kept messages: [ 'user' ]
orphaned toolCall blocks remaining: 0
PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The aborted assistant turn containing `toolu_c8b41a5a51b6469b8f2aa84c` is dropped by `repairToolUseResultPairing({ erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop" })` before reaching `summarizeInStages`. No orphaned `toolCall` blocks remain in the kept messages. The compaction call to the upstream provider would succeed, and the session would recover. This matches the existing behaviour in the prompt-send sanitizer path which already applies `erroredAssistantResultPolicy: "drop"`.

- **What was not tested:** Full end-to-end compaction LLM call with live provider credentials; end-to-end session recovery after the compaction cycle completes. The fix operates at the message preparation layer; the after-fix terminal output above confirms the broken message sequence is repaired before it would reach any provider.

commands.author: yetval

target.title: fix(cron): preserve startup overflow catch-up deferrals in start() maintenance pass
target.body:
## Summary
After the gateway is down across one or more cron slots and restarts with more overdue cron-expression jobs than `maxMissedJobsPerRestart` (default 5), the overflow jobs have their startup catch-up run silently dropped for a full schedule period. A daily `0 9 * * *` job whose catch-up was staggered to `now + 5s` is pushed back to the next natural `09:00`, roughly 16 hours later, so the missed run never happens.

## Root cause
`runMissedJobs` defers overflow jobs to a near-future staggered slot and deliberately protects those deferrals so a later maintenance pass does not advance them. The protective pass in `src/cron/service/timer.ts:1856` disables future-slot repair:

```ts
// timer.ts, the sibling protective pass during catch-up
recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance(state, { repairFutureCronNextRunAtMs: false });
```

But `start()`'s own second maintenance pass in `src/cron/service/ops.ts:269` omitted any protection:

```ts
// before (ops.ts:269)
const changed = recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance(state, { recomputeExpired: true });
```

`repairFutureCronNextRunAtMs` defaults to `true` (`src/cron/service/jobs.ts:675`), so `shouldRepairFutureCronNextRunAtMs` (`src/cron/service/jobs.ts:197`) sees each overflow cron deferral as a stale future slot (`now < nextRunAtMs < naturalNext`, `kind: "cron"`, non-`agentTurn`) and recomputes it to its natural schedule slot, clobbering the catch-up.

## Fix
The right fix is not to disable all future-slot repair in `start()`'s pass (that pass legitimately repairs genuinely stale future cron slots on startup). It is to exempt only the jobs that `runMissedJobs` just deferred, while leaving repair on for everything else.

- `runMissedJobs` now returns the set of job ids it deferred this startup (`src/cron/service/timer.ts`).
- `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance` gains a `skipFutureRepairJobIds` option that skips future-slot repair for exactly those ids while still running every other repair/expiry branch (`src/cron/service/jobs.ts`).
- `start()` threads the deferred ids into its second pass (`src/cron/service/ops.ts:269`):

```ts
// after
const deferredCatchupJobIds = await runMissedJobs(state, { ... });
...
const changed = recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance(state, {
  recomputeExpired: true,
  skipFutureRepairJobIds: deferredCatchupJobIds,
});
```

An earlier revision of this PR used a blanket `repairFutureCronNextRunAtMs: false` here; that over-corrected by disabling ordinary stale-future repair for every job in the pass. The scoped exemption fixes the dropped catch-up without weakening the repair feature.

## Why this is the right boundary
The catch-up deferral and its protection are owned by the startup path. `runMissedJobs` creates the deferrals, so it is the natural owner of "which ids are exempt this startup," and `start()`'s follow-up pass is the only consumer. The exemption is keyed on those ids, so:
- overflow catch-up deferrals survive until their staggered tick, and
- any other stale future cron slot present at startup is still repaired by the same pass.

No sibling surfaces share the defect: `runMissedJobs` (timer.ts) already disables repair in its own pass, and regular timer-tick maintenance uses `recomputeNextRuns`/read paths outside the startup overflow window. Existing behavior for missing, expired, and already-executed slots is preserved.

## Verification
New colocated regression tests `src/cron/service.startup-overflow-clobber.test.ts` drive the real `ops.start()`:
- `keeps the overflow daily-cron catch-up deferral after start()'s maintenance pass` (6 overdue cron jobs at production defaults: `maxMissedJobsPerRestart` 5, stagger 5000ms). Fails on pristine `main`, passes with this patch.
- `still repairs a stale future cron slot on start() when no jobs were deferred`. Passes with this patch; fails if the exemption is widened back to a blanket `repairFutureCronNextRunAtMs: false`, guarding the scoping.

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.startup-overflow-clobber.test.ts
  -> Tests 2 passed (2)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
  src/cron/service/ops.test.ts src/cron/service/ops.regression.test.ts \
  src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts src/cron/service/timer.test.ts
  -> Test Files 6 passed (6), Tests 114 passed (114)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.jobs.test.ts src/cron/stagger.test.ts (+3 jobs suites)
  -> Test Files 5 passed (5), Tests 97 passed (97)
oxlint --type-aware <changed files>  -> exit 0
oxfmt --check <changed files>        -> All matched files use the correct format
run-tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json                      -> exit 0
run-tsgo -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json   -> exit 0
```

Proof matrix across all three variants of the same two tests:

| variant | overflow deferral test | stale-future repair test |
| --- | --- | --- |
| pristine `main` | FAIL (clobbered) | PASS |
| blanket `repairFutureCronNextRunAtMs: false` | PASS | FAIL (ordinary repair lost) |
| this PR (scoped exemption) | PASS | PASS |

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: overflow cron catch-up deferral survives `start()`'s maintenance pass while ordinary stale-future repair still runs.
Real environment tested: the real `ops.start()` startup path (recover, `runMissedJobs`, second maintenance pass, arm timer) over temp cron stores, on pristine `main` and on the patched tree with identical inputs. Output below is captured from the cron service's own structured logger plus the resulting `nextRunAtMs`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: drive `ops.start()` with a capturing logger over two stores (6 overdue cron jobs; one stale-future cron slot).
Evidence after fix:
```
# BEFORE (pristine main f94a2506d2) - Scenario A: 6 overdue cron jobs, production defaults
[info] cron: staggering missed jobs to prevent gateway overload {"immediateCount":5,"deferredCount":1,"totalMissed":6}
[info] cron: running missed jobs after restart {"count":5,"jobIds":["hourly-0",...,"hourly-4"]}
daily-overflow nextRunAtMs = 1765702800000 (2025-12-14T09:00:00.000Z)   <- catch-up dropped, next run ~16h away

# AFTER (this patch) - Scenario A: identical inputs
[info] cron: staggering missed jobs to prevent gateway overload {"immediateCount":5,"deferredCount":1,"totalMissed":6}
[info] cron: running missed jobs after restart {"count":5,"jobIds":["hourly-0",...,"hourly-4"]}
[info] cron: started {"enabled":true,"jobs":6,"nextWakeAtMs":1765645205000}
daily-overflow nextRunAtMs = 1765645205000 (2025-12-13T17:00:05.000Z)    <- catch-up preserved, gateway wakes in 5s

# AFTER (this patch) - Scenario B: 1 stale future cron slot, no overflow deferrals
[info] cron: started {"enabled":true,"jobs":1,"nextWakeAtMs":1765702800000}
daily-stale-future nextRunAtMs = 1765702800000 (2025-12-14T09:00:00.000Z) <- stale slot still repaired to natural slot
```
Observed result after fix: the overflow daily job keeps its `now + 5s` catch-up (timer arms to wake in 5s), and a non-deferred stale future slot is still repaired to its natural `09:00`.
What was not tested: end-to-end gateway restart against a live on-disk store with the OS timer actually firing the deferred run; the proof drives the startup function directly under a fixed clock.

## Relation to existing work
PR #78272 (merged) added the future-slot repair feature and the protective pass in `timer.ts`, but did not touch `ops.ts`, leaving its second pass unprotected. PR #81731 (open) fixes a separate exact-second slot misclassification in `jobs.ts` and does not change `ops.ts:269`; the two changes do not overlap.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): Add group admin privacy controls
target.body:
Closes #69233

## What changed

This adds an optional per-group WhatsApp admin override via `channels.whatsapp.groups.<jid>.admin` and wildcard `channels.whatsapp.groups."*".admin`.

When an admin is configured for a group scope:
- the configured E.164 number bypasses mention gating for that group
- non-admin group members cannot run slash commands in that group
- the configured admin remains able to run slash commands for that group

When no admin is configured, the existing group command authorization behavior is preserved.

## Why

The issue described a privacy gap in mixed-trust WhatsApp groups: non-admin members could still probe bot behavior through mentions and slash commands. The first pass of this feature covered the basic config and enforcement, but upstream readiness also needed the behavior pinned down for wildcard group config and for the no-admin-configured fallback path.

## User impact

Users can now designate a WhatsApp group admin by phone number and use that as a privacy boundary for command discovery in group chats.

This is intentionally backwards-compatible:
- groups without `admin` continue using the existing command authorization rules
- exact-group and wildcard group config both work
- docs now describe the new config key

## Root cause

OpenClaw already had group policy, allowlists, and mention gating, but it did not have a per-group owner/admin concept for WhatsApp. That meant there was no narrow way to let a trusted operator manage the bot in a group without exposing command access to other participants covered by broader allowlist rules.

## Validation

- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src/group-policy.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/access-control.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-activation.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- tsgo:core`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- tsgo:extensions`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- check`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- build`

## AI assistance

- AI-assisted: yes, with Codex
- Testing: fully tested locally with the commands listed above
- Bot review threads addressed and resolved after follow-up fixes

target.title: fix(memory): improve error message when node:sqlite is unavailable
target.body:
## Summary

- Keeps the existing PR branch scoped to `extensions/memory-core`.
- Classifies `node:sqlite` / missing built-in sqlite runtime errors in `buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult(error, overrides?)`.
- Keeps the existing quota and generic branches, and keeps explicit `warning` / `action` overrides authoritative.
- Fixes the latest CI lint finding by using `String#includes()` for the sqlite-missing checks.

## Behavior

When the runtime cannot load `node:sqlite`, `memory_search` now returns the same unavailable result shape with a targeted warning/action instead of the generic embedding-provider message.

The action matches current OpenClaw support policy: Node.js `>=22.19.0`, with Node.js 24 recommended. The doc link uses the valid Memory CLI page: https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli/memory.

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `memory_search` unavailable payloads now classify missing `node:sqlite` runtime errors with targeted warning/action text while preserving quota, generic, and explicit override behavior.
- Real environment tested: Windows PowerShell in the OpenClaw repo; old-runtime missing-builtin reproduction plus Node.js v24.13.0 for helper output, tests, and lint.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node -e "try { require('node:sqlite'); console.log('node:sqlite available'); } catch (error) { console.log(String(error.message)); }"`; `node --import tsx -e "import { buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult } from './extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts'; ..."`; `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts`; `node node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts`; `node node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output below shows the missing builtin error, the after-fix unavailable payload, override preservation, and targeted test/lint output.
- Observed result after fix: A missing `node:sqlite` error returns `disabled: true`, `unavailable: true`, the original error string, and targeted Node.js upgrade guidance; custom `warning` and `action` overrides still win; the targeted memory-core tests pass.
- What was not tested: No known gaps for this helper-level unavailable payload change; a full old-Node live memory index/search session was not run because the focused bug is the formatter branch and the old-runtime reproduction plus helper output exercise that behavior directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The old-runtime missing-builtin command demonstrates the source error; the current supported Node.js 24 runtime is used for the branch output and tests.

Old-runtime reproduction of the missing builtin:

```text
$ node -e "try { require('node:sqlite'); console.log('node:sqlite available'); } catch (error) { console.log(String(error.message)); }"
No such built-in module: node:sqlite
```

Current branch unavailable payload for that error:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e "import { buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult } from './extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts'; console.log(JSON.stringify(buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult('No such built-in module: node:sqlite'), null, 2));"
{
  "results": [],
  "disabled": true,
  "unavailable": true,
  "error": "No such built-in module: node:sqlite",
  "warning": "Memory search is unavailable because node:sqlite is not available in this Node.js runtime.",
  "action": "Use OpenClaw with Node.js 22.19.0 or newer; Node.js 24 is recommended for memory search. See https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli/memory.",
  "debug": {
    "warning": "Memory search is unavailable because node:sqlite is not available in this Node.js runtime.",
    "action": "Use OpenClaw with Node.js 22.19.0 or newer; Node.js 24 is recommended for memory search. See https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli/memory.",
    "error": "No such built-in module: node:sqlite"
  }
}
```

Override preservation check:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e "import { buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult } from './extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts'; console.log(JSON.stringify(buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult('No such built-in module: node:sqlite', { warning: 'custom warning', action: 'custom action' }), null, 2));"
{
  "results": [],
  "disabled": true,
  "unavailable": true,
  "error": "No such built-in module: node:sqlite",
  "warning": "custom warning",
  "action": "custom action",
  "debug": {
    "warning": "custom warning",
    "action": "custom action",
    "error": "No such built-in module: node:sqlite"
  }
}
```

Note: `node scripts/run-bundled-extension-oxlint.mjs` and `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8` both timed out locally on Windows after 5 minutes with no diagnostics, but the CI failure was the two `prefer-includes` findings in `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts`; the changed file and memory-core extension path now pass oxlint directly.

target.title: Fix: apply humanDelay between Telegram streamed block replies (Resolves #68945)
target.body:
Fixes #68945.

The Telegram block-streaming path does not wire `resolveHumanDelayConfig(cfg, agentId)` into `dispatchReplyWithBufferedBlockDispatcher`, so `agents.defaults.humanDelay` / per-agent `humanDelay` overrides have no effect on Telegram block replies.

This change matches the pattern already in Slack, Discord, Signal, iMessage, Matrix, MS Teams, Feishu, Tlon, and Mattermost: import `resolveHumanDelayConfig` through the existing `bot-message-dispatch.agent.runtime.ts` seam and pass its result as `dispatcherOptions.humanDelay` in `bot-message-dispatch.ts`.

## Test plan

target.title: feat: add gateway.nodes.invokeTimeoutMs config option
target.body:
Fixes #68090

## Summary

The `node.invoke` timeout was hardcoded to 30 seconds with no way to configure it. Users running long operations through node commands had to accept the fixed timeout.

## Changes

1. **`src/config/types.gateway.ts`** — Added `invokeTimeoutMs?: number` to `GatewayNodesConfig`
2. **`src/gateway/node-registry.ts`** — Added `defaultInvokeTimeoutMs` property (default 30000), used as fallback when no per-call timeout is specified
3. **`src/gateway/server.impl.ts`** — Wires `gateway.nodes.invokeTimeoutMs` config to the node registry at startup

## Usage

```json
{
  "gateway": {
    "nodes": {
      "invokeTimeoutMs": 60000
    }
  }
}
```

## Test Plan

- Without config: verify default 30s timeout behavior is unchanged
- With `gateway.nodes.invokeTimeoutMs: 60000`: verify node.invoke uses 60s timeout
- With per-call `timeoutMs`: verify per-call value takes precedence over config default
